what else would you add to the list?

not sure where to start with personal finances? comment “GUIDE” to receive a free checklist and tools to improve your financial life 🥳

#personalfinance #twenties #financialeducation #financialresponsibility #money
items.transcript: If you don't want to be broke in your 30s, here are four things you need to start doing in your 20s. Most people will ignore this, but if you start now, you'll come out ahead. I'm Lillian and I share tips on how to level up your finances in your 20s, so if that's your goal, drop a follow. The first thing I do is avoiding buy now pay later like the plague. Although breaking up a purchase into four sounds enticing, it can be a really slippery slope and you don't want to be that person who orders DoorDash using Farna. If it's not a house or a car, I would avoid putting stuff on installment payments. Second is living within your means and this means not upgrading everything in your life just because of a raise or increase in income. And this is not me saying to not upgrade anything. I've definitely upgraded my life when my income went up. Like if I got a $10,000 raise, I would spend $2,000 on stuff I wanted and put $8,000 into savings and investments. Third is investing a little every month because a little goes a long way. I started investing in college in diversified index funds when I didn't have much money and it's been five years since I've invested and my portfolio has reached over six figures. When you're young, the most powerful currency you have is time and every bit that you invest now will be worth so much more when you're older. Fourth is building multiple income streams. With the current economy, relying on one paycheck is actually pretty risky. I am personally doing this by extracting the value I get from my nine to five and funneling the savings from my paychecks into investments and assets that can generate me more income. You can also use the skills that you learn from your nine to five and build something on the side that you own and that you have equity in. And that's exactly what I'm doing with my online business.

items.caption:
what are your travel nonnegotiables? ✈️ 

#travel #expensivethings #worthit #productreview #money @capitalone @americanexpress @laneige_us
items.transcript: Expensive things that are worth the money travel edition. These are my travel non-negotiables as someone in my mid-20s who's been a seasoned traveler. First one on the list is having TSA PreCheck and Global Entry. If there's anything about me, I am taking every shortcut I can get at the airport. When you have TSA PreCheck, there is a separate line that you can go through. Your ID is not checked because it's already gone through a system. The lines are generally shorter. And when it comes to coming back into the States and standing in customs lines, I am going to Global Entry. It takes literally less than a minute to get out the customs line. What a lot of people don't know is that if you sign up for Global Entry, TSA PreCheck is automatically included. You do one interview with law enforcement for a background check and that lasts for five years. Some credit cards offer Global Entry as a reward or you can sign up on the Global Entry website. Second, I am only taking non-stop flights. Back in college when I wanted to save money, I would take the cheapest flight possible. But as I got older, you realize that time is money and the time spent taking the cheapest airfare and having to stop before your next destination is just too much of a time sum. Third thing on the list is premium economy at a minimum for cross-ocean plane trips. A lot of airlines lately have less and less standard service for just regular economy and when it comes to long-haul flights, I want to be more comfortable. I want to be able to recline my seat a little bit more, have a little bit of legroom.

items.caption:
👇🏼 My top 3 fund holdings:

Fund 1: VTI
* Stands for Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF
* Tracks all of the publicly traded companies in the U.S. (~3,500 stocks) so you are holding a small piece of each
* Top represented holdings: NVIDIA, AAPL, MSFT, AMZN, GOOGL
* Expense ratio: 0.03% (very low!)
* Annual dividend: $3.77/share

Fund 2: QQQM
* Stands for Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF
* Tracks the top 100 non-financial companies globally (mostly tech-heavy)
* Expense ratio: 0.15%
* More liquid & better expense ratio than QQQ
* Annual dividend: $1.27/share

Fund 3: VLXVX
* Stands for Vanguard Target Retirement 2065 Fund
* This is a retirement-focused fund for those with a target retirement year of 2065
* Allocation of stocks and bonds are adjusted automatically over time (it becomes more bond-heavy/conservative gradually), so you don’t have to manually rebalance your portfolio
* Widely used in 401k, 403b, and HSA plans but could also be found in most major brokerages
* Expense ratio: 0.08%
* Annual dividend: $0.80/share

💬 Comment “YES” to see my portfolio holdings & a list of money tools to organize and grow your portfolio 

Please ensure to do your own research! This is for informative purposes only, as everyone’s risk profiles are different 🤍

Context: I’ve been consistently investing my earnings since I was 20, and now I’m 26. I started off putting in small amounts and increased my contributions as my earnings increased. A majority of the $ came from my job and my business, but capital gains account for around 20-30% of my overall portfolio depending on the market ^^ My investing approach is passive and holding on for the long term!

Follow for more content about business & investing 💚

items.transcript: Expensive things that are worth the money that will improve your life and productivity. The first thing on my list is the Apple Studio Display Monitor. I love this so much. It's one of my favorite things I've ever bought. It works super well with the Mac ecosystem and it makes me want to do work and I love watching movies and TV shows on this monitor. Second is investing in a high quality work bag. I actually only used a backpack for the longest time and until I invested in a nice leather work bag, I didn't realize how much it affected my mood going to work. I only have one nice work bag. It's the Kiana Double Loop Bag. It's super cute. The leather is real Italian leather. Even though it was a little bit pricey, it has this really nice suede feeling and it feels like it just holds up very well. I also like how it's more of a relaxed look and you can put your water bottle, you can put a lot of stuff in here. So this is a bag that I'll be using for a very long time. The third item on my list is a robot vacuum and this is life changing. If you're anything like me and you hate cleaning, then the robot vacuum pretty much does the vacuuming for you in the background while you relax, while you do work. You do have to set up the robot vacuum and clean it between its cleans, but it's been well worth the investment.

items.caption:
#HotelsDotComAmbassador Is this surprising?? 🤭🗽 

You can find hotels like these and many more on @hotelsdotcom, for both work and personal trips! They have this cool feature called Save Your Way where you can choose to use member savings now OR turn it into future travel rewards (but check your company travel policy first)! If you’re booking work trips you can save up for your dream vacation with the rewards 👀🏖️

Get access to Save Your Way immediately after making a free account on Hotels.com~

#worktrip #travel #businesstrip #traveltips
items.transcript: This is unreal, but I'm moving from SF to New York City! I'm actually just going there for a business trip, but I wonder what it'd be like to live a bi-coastal life. I took a red-eye this time, which I will not be doing again, and after a full day of working in the office, I checked into my hotel, which is also on Hotels.com. And for anyone who books their own work travel, they have this feature called Saver Way where you can choose to use your member savings now or turn it into future travel rewards. But check your company travel policy first. I recently switched teams at my company working in product marketing, and this is my second work trip within my first two months on this new team. I've traveled to a good deal of conferences for work to see the products that I work on be displayed for customers to see, which is pretty cool. I can't believe it's been four years since I've been working, and it honestly feels like I graduated yesterday. Work trips are usually so jam-packed, but I made sure to check out some iconic spots and to meet up with some friends who live here. I can't wait to be back and check out Hotels.com to book your next trip.

items.caption:
Results of investing consistently for almost 6 years! I didn’t have a lot while starting out but the habit does add up. 80% of my portfolio is in funds, investing for a long time horizon, and relatively high risk tolerance ^^

#investing #personalfinance #wealthcreation #etf #investmentportfolio
items.transcript: I built my portfolio to seven figures at 26 years old, and these are the top three ETFs that I hold in my portfolio. I've been investing consistently for almost six years. And if you want to learn tips on how to level up your finances, be sure to follow me for more. My biggest holding by far is VTI, which stands for Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF. I like this fund because it has a low expense ratio, meaning that there are very little management fees, and it tracks the stock of all publicly traded companies in the US stock market, so you don't have to pick and choose companies to invest in. My second top holding is QQQM, which stands for Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF. This ETF tracks the top 100 non-financial companies globally that are doing a lot of tech innovation. So naturally, a lot of the companies on this list are heavily weighted in the tech sector. Even though VTI in the US economy as a whole is heavily weighted on tech already, I am personally very bullish on the tech industry, as we've recently seen with AI. My third top holding is VLXVX, and this stands for the Vanguard Target Retirement 2065 Fund. This fund is in my 401k account, and it's essentially a mix of stocks and bonds. And what I like about this fund is that when you are young, it's mostly going to be allocated in high-growth stocks with a little bit of bonds, and as you age, it will become more and more conservative. I don't have to go and manually change the allocation, so I like that hands-off approach.
